nativeRange.habitat: Slow-moving and standing waters of floodplain lakes, lagoons, and river margins over fine sand and silt substrates; often among leaf litter and submerged roots in shaded areas

decorPreferences: deep fine sand substrate (minimum 5 cm) for sifting behaviour
driftwood and twisted roots for shelter and territory demarcation
leaf litter (Indian almond, oak) to mimic natural biotope
dim or moderate lighting; floating plants to diffuse light
smooth flat rocks or overhanging cave structures for spawning sites
sparse planting with robust rooted or epiphyte species (Anubias, Java fern) anchored to hardscape to avoid uprooting during sand-sifting

diet.notes: Obligate sift-feeder in the wild: takes mouthfuls of sand and sifts out small invertebrates, worms, microcrustaceans, and organic detritus. In the aquarium, accepts sinking micro-pellets, Repashy gel foods, frozen and live bloodworms, daphnia, cyclops, and baby brine shrimp. Foods must reach the substrate — surface or mid-water feeding is largely ignored. Variety is important; an exclusive bloodworm diet is associated with bloat. Supplement with high-quality sinking granules to ensure complete nutrition.

goodWith: small to medium peaceful tetras (cardinal, rummy-nose)
small Corydoras catfish (C. sterbai, C. aeneus) with shared temperature tolerance
Apistogramma and other small dwarf cichlids (with space)
avoidWith: boisterous fast-swimming fish that outcompete at feeding time
gravel-substrate fish incompatible with mandatory sand setup

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase photo gallery for Biotodoma cupido (species ID 6440); may contain CC-licensed photos from contributors.

summary: The Cupid Cichlid is a small (~12 cm), sand-sifting Geophagine cichlid from the Amazon basin. It requires a deep fine sand substrate for its natural sift-feeding behaviour, soft acidic water, and a monogamous pair bond. Cave-spawner; intermediate care.
careNotes: Biotodoma cupido is one of the smaller Geophagine (eartheater) cichlids, closely related to Geophagus but more compact, reaching roughly 9–12 cm. It is endemic to the Amazon basin, where it inhabits slow-moving or standing warm, soft, acidic waters over fine sand. In captivity it requires a minimum of 5 cm of fine play sand — the species sifts mouthfuls of substrate to extract food particles in the manner typical of Geophagines, and cannot perform this behaviour on gravel. Without adequate sand depth, specimens become stressed and often refuse to feed properly. Water chemistry should replicate the soft, acidic conditions of its native range: pH 5.5–7.2, gH 1–10, temperature 26–30 °C. Harder or more alkaline water is associated with chronic stress and shortened lifespan. The tank should be at least 150 L for a breeding pair, with 200 L or more recommended if housed with community tankmates. Decorate with driftwood, leaf litter (Indian almond leaves are ideal), and robust epiphyte plants anchored to hardscape, as sifting behaviour will displace loose substrate and uproot unweighted plants. Biotodoma cupido is distinguishable from the closely related B. wavrini by its more compact, deeper-bodied shape; B. wavrini is notably more elongated. Maintain the species as a committed pair — they form monogamous bonds and are best purchased as a mated or compatible pair. Conspecific aggression is low outside of spawning defence, but the species is sensitive to chronic harassment from boisterous tankmates and should be housed with calm, similarly-sized community fish.
breedingNotes: Biotodoma cupido is a substrate spawner (cave spawner) that forms a monogamous pair bond. Conditioning with a varied live and frozen diet stimulates breeding behaviour. The pair selects a sheltered spawning site — typically under overhanging driftwood, a flat rock cave, or a clay pot — and cleans the surface before depositing adhesive eggs. Both parents participate in brood defence and guard the larvae actively. Water temperature at the upper end of the range (28–30 °C) and very soft, slightly acidic water promotes spawning. After hatching (approximately 48–72 hours), larvae are moved to pits dug in the sand. Fry are free-swimming after approximately 5–7 days and can accept micro-foods such as baby brine shrimp nauplii and micro-worms. Breeding difficulty is moderate — conditioning and correct water chemistry are key barriers rather than unusual husbandry requirements.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Biotodoma cupido
primary.notes: Primary source. Comprehensive species profile covering taxonomy, native range (Amazon basin, floodplain lakes and river margins), water parameters (24–30 °C, pH 5.0–7.5, hardness to 15 °H), adult size (~12 cm), diet (sift-feeder; accepts frozen and dried foods), behaviour, compatibility, and breeding (substrate spawner, monogamous, biparental brood care). Distinguishes B. cupido (deeper-bodied) from B. wavrini (more elongated).

additional.name: FishBase — Biotodoma cupido
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (to 12 cm TL), native range (Amazon and Orinoco basins, Brazil, Peru, Colombia, Venezuela), taxonomy (Cichlidae, Cichliformes), climate (tropical), and depth. Confirms detritivore/invertivore feeding mode consistent with Geophagine sifting behaviour.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Eartheater Cichlid Care Guide
additional.notes: General Geophagine/eartheater care guide. Confirms mandatory fine sand substrate for sifting behaviour, soft acidic water preference, diet (sinking foods reaching substrate), and community compatibility with peaceful tankmates. Biotodoma specifically mentioned in context of smaller Geophagines.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Eartheater Cichlids Feature
additional.notes: Feature article on Geophagine cichlids. Covers Biotodoma cupido care, its compact body vs. B. wavrini, sand-sifting requirement, and cave-spawning biparental care. Water parameters and tank setup notes consistent with SeriouslyFish profile.

additional.name: Cichlid-Forum — Biotodoma cupido species profile
additional.notes: Hobbyist cichlid community species profile. Confirms monogamous pairing, biparental brood care, soft acidic water requirement, minimum sand depth for sifting, and distinguishing morphological features from B. wavrini. Breeding notes on pit-digging larval care consistent with other sources.

fish.breedingNotes: Cave-spawner; forms lifelong monogamous pair. Both parents defend eggs and larvae. Conditioning with varied live/frozen foods and soft, warm water (28–30 °C, pH 6.0–6.8) triggers spawning. Fry accept baby brine shrimp nauplii and micro-worms when free-swimming (~5–7 days post-hatch).
